name: Kalalau Trail to Hanakāpīʻai Beach
highlights: Most iconic trail in Hawaii; first 2 mi to beach open to day hikers; advance reservation required via gohaena.com
Dramatic Nā Pali Coast cliffs and ocean views the entire way
The beach at Hanakāpīʻai is wild, remote, and unforgettable
Limited daily permits keep the trail uncrowded — book early
bookingWindow: Advance reservation required via gohaena.com — book as early as possible

name: Awaʻawapuhi Trail
highlights: Finest ridge trail on the island; knife-edge viewpoint 2,500 ft above Nā Pali Coast
The descent through native forest is lush and quiet — the viewpoint at the end is staggering
No permit required; far fewer hikers than the Kalalau Trail
The return climb is the crux — save energy for the uphill back

name: Pihea Trail (Kōkeʻe to Alakaʻi Swamp)
highlights: World's highest tropical swamp at 4,000 ft; rare endemic birds (apapane, iiwi, pueo)
Boardwalk section through the Alakaʻi Swamp is otherworldly — dense moss, cloud forest, endemic plants
Views into Kalalau Valley from the Pihea overlook at the trailhead
Can be muddy — sturdy footwear essential

name: Sleeping Giant (Nounou Mountain)
highlights: Local's hike above Kapaa; panoramic views; less crowded than north shore trails
Summit views stretch from the ocean to the interior mountains
Three trailhead options (east, west, Kuamoo) — east side is most popular
Best done early morning before the trade winds and heat build

name: Waimea Canyon Trail
location: Waimea Canyon State Park, West Side
highlights: Primary canyon trail along the rim; detour to top of Waipoo Falls (800 ft cascade)
The Grand Canyon of the Pacific — deep red and green canyon walls, layered volcanic geology
Multiple lookout points along the canyon rim road for shorter visits
Cooler temperatures at elevation make this a good midday hike

name: Surfing — Hanalei Bay
highlights: The most beautiful bay in Hawaii — surfing here is a genuinely elemental experience
Summer (May–September) is the beginner season — small, consistent swell, warm water, long slow rides
Multiple surf schools operate from the pier end; lessons run 2 hours with board and wetsuit included
Winter swells (November–March) make this an expert-only break — come to watch, not paddle out
operator: Hanalei Surf School / Hawaiian Surfing Adventures
bookingWindow: Book lessons in advance; walk-in possible in shoulder season

name: Nā Pali Coast — Boat Tour
location: Departs Port Allen (South Shore) or Hanalei (North Shore)
highlights: The only way to see Nā Pali's sea caves, waterfalls, and 4,000-foot cliffs from the water
Spinner dolphins escort boats regularly; humpback whales visible November–March
Captain Andy's and Blue Dolphin are the established operators
North shore departures (Hanalei) run May–September only; south shore (Port Allen) year-round
bookingWindow: Book at captainandy.com — popular tours sell out weeks ahead in summer

name: Ke Ala Hele Makalae Coastal Path
location: East Shore — Lydgate Beach Park to Anahola, 16+ miles
highlights: Paved multi-use coastal path with ocean views the entire way — one of the best cycling corridors in Hawaii
Flat, accessible, family-friendly; passes Lydgate Beach Park, Kapaa town, and several snorkel spots
Bike rentals available in Kapaa town within steps of the path; e-bikes also available
Early morning before trade winds pick up is the best window
bookingWindow: Self-guided — bike rental from Coconut Coasters in Kapaa or similar

name: Waimea Canyon Road — Cycling
location: Waimea → Koke'e State Park, West Side
highlights: The road climbs 3,600 feet from sea level to the canyon rim — the Pacific Ocean visible over your shoulder the entire way
Light vehicle traffic outside peak visitor hours; the descent is exceptional
At the top: Waimea Canyon Lookout (the Grand Canyon of the Pacific) and Koke'e State Park
Road bike or hybrid required — the grade is sustained throughout
bookingWindow: Self-guided — no permit. Bike rental from Kapaa or Poipu shops.
